game_name: Dungeons & Dragons: Eye of the Beholder

design_philosophy.core_loop: First-person dungeon exploration with turn-based tactical combat; descend through multi-level dungeons defeating monsters, solving puzzles, recruiting NPCs, managing party of up to 6 characters
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource attrition (health, spells, supplies) balanced against unforgiving combat encounters and puzzle-solving requirements; party vulnerability in narrow corridors against ranged enemies
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Leading a tabletop D&D adventuring party through a dangerous dungeon; faithful adaptation of AD&D rules and spirit with graphics/audio immersion
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Uncompromising difficulty and puzzle-solving focus over story exposition; favors tactical preparation and resource management over narrative hand-holding; uses point-and-click interface to create authentic D&D mechanical feel rather than action-game convenience

difficulty.lives_system: Party death is permanent unless characters can be resurrected via NPC interaction or skeleton resurrection; loss of party members represents irreversible progression cost
difficulty.checkpointing: Save system exists but respects dungeon integrity; dungeons do not reset on reload, maintaining resource attrition tension
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Party composition flexibility (recruit NPCs, dismiss characters, resurrect skeletons) allows difficulty modulation; spell preparation and item inventory management create tactical decision-making space

progression.upgrade_system: Character leveling via experience; equipment loot from defeated enemies; spell acquisition through dungeon exploration and NPC recruitment; character class defines available spell schools
progression.unlock_mechanism: Descending dungeon levels unlocks access to deeper areas and harder encounters; NPC recruitment unlocks party slots (up to 6 total); spell access tied to character class and dungeon discovery
replayability_hooks: Party import feature allows carrying characters from Eye of the Beholder I into sequels (Legend of Darkmoon, Assault on Myth Drannor)
Variable NPC recruitment creates different party compositions across playthroughs
Puzzle solutions may be attempted in multiple ways depending on party skills

world_building_techniques: First-person dungeon visualization establishes atmosphere and spatial storytelling
NPC dialogue provides quest context and lore fragments during recruitment
Dungeon layout progression (sewers → dwarf clans → drow clans → beholder lair) mirrors narrative escalation
Enemy encounters embedded in environment rather than exposition

boss_design.philosophy: Final encounter (Xanathar) represents culmination of dungeon descent rather than narrative climax; boss death triggers minimal narrative closure (text window only in original DOS version; animated ending added in Amiga port)
notable_bosses: Xanathar (eponymous beholder, final boss) - serves as dungeon's controlling force causing initial cave-in; defeat unblocks surface return

faction_indicators: Drow clans (dungeon inhabitants, representing underground opposition)
Dwarf clans (dungeon inhabitants, potential allies or obstacles)
danger_signals: Cave collapse caused by Xanathar (environmental threat establishing urgency)
Monster encounters increasing in difficulty/density with depth
Party vulnerability in narrow corridor combat scenarios against ranged enemies
Permanent character death mechanic (raises stakes of dungeon exploration)
environmental_storytelling: Dungeon architecture progresses from sewers (familiar/civilized) to alien depths (drow/dwarf territories, then beholder lair)
NPC corpses available for resurrection reflect dungeon's lethal history
Monster placement and type variation indicates territorial control by different factions
Loot distribution suggests previous adventurers' attempts and failures

sources_used: wikipedia: Eye of the Beholder (video game)
